context: Turn to the book of Jude, and as you turn to the book of Jude, it's only one chapter, but what a power-packed chapter of God's word this is.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it says in verse three that it was needful for me, right in the middle of the verse, to write unto you and to exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: Jude wrote to exhort believers to earnestly contend for the faith once delivered to the saints because false teachers had crept in unawares, corrupting grace and denying Christ.

context: because in verse number four, there are certain men crept in unawares who were before of old ordained to this condemnation, ungodly men, turning the grace of our God into lasciviousness and denying the only Lord God and our Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Jude wrote to urge believers to contend for the faith precisely because ungodly men had crept in unawares, turning grace into lasciviousness and denying Christ—the very threat requiring the epistle's warning.

context: keep yourselves in the love of God, looking for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ unto eternal life
point_summary: Keeping oneself in the love of God is presented not as passive security but as an active, ongoing discipline maintained in the face of false teaching and moral opposition.

context: and of some, have compassion making a difference
point_summary: Jude 1:22 grounds the call to have compassion on those who doubt, while recognizing that the approach to sharing the gospel must vary with each individual, though the message itself never changes.

context: and others, save with fear, pulling them out of the fire, hating even the garment spotted by the flesh
point_summary: Jude 1:23 is cited to show that rescuing the lost requires urgency and fear, and that the method of gospel engagement must vary with the individual even as the message remains unchanged.

context: ...the Bible says in verse 22, and of some, have compassion...
point_summary: Jude 1:22 calls believers to have compassion on the lost, recognizing that gospel outreach requires adapting one's approach—not the message—to the individual, rather than viewing unbelievers as enemies.

context: beloved, when I gave all diligence to write unto you the common salvation, it was needful for me to write unto you and to exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith, which was once delivered unto the saints
point_summary: The urgency to "earnestly contend for the faith once delivered to the saints" is grounded in the ongoing reality that false teachers continue infiltrating the church across every generation, just as in the first century.

context: one chapter, verse number three, it says that you would earnestly contend for the faith.
point_summary: Believers bear a God-given responsibility to earnestly contend for the faith once delivered, honoring those who sacrificed to preserve it and guarding against false teaching in the present.

context: Remember what Jude says, that certain men have crept in unawares, all right? And so they creep in to devour, to destroy, to turn the grace of God, according to Jude, into lasciviousness...
point_summary: Certain men who creep in unawares, according to Jude, represent the external danger to the church by perverting grace into lasciviousness.

context: Beloved, when I gave all diligence to write unto you of the common salvation, then notice where he says, it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: Jude's phrase "the faith once delivered to the saints" is cited as evidence that the body of Christian doctrine is a fixed, unified deposit worth earnestly contending for.

context: You read the book of Jude, I think it's verse number 9, when he does bring a railing accusation about the body of Moses, he said the Lord rebuked thee.
point_summary: Even the archangel Michael, when confronting Satan over Moses' body, deferred judgment to the Lord rather than railing—illustrating that withholding forgiveness gives the devil an opening that believers must refuse to leave.

context: Jude, verse number 3 and 4, that had crept in unawares, that turned the grace of God into lasciviousness.
point_summary: False teaching's primary danger to the saved is not loss of salvation but loss of gospel effectiveness, making Jude's urgent warning against those who crept in unawares directly relevant to continued fruitfulness.

context: Jude, verse number 3 and 4, that had crept in unawares, that turned the grace of God into lasciviousness.
point_summary: False teachers who "crept in unawares" and turned grace into lasciviousness (Jude 3–4) threaten not a believer's eternal security but their doctrinal stability and gospel effectiveness.

context: we're to keep ourselves in the love of God
point_summary: Keeping oneself in the love of God (Jude 1:21) is cited as the believer's responsive duty, grounded in the prior reality that God's agape love—demonstrated at the cross—cannot be separated from his own.

context: The Bible says in the book of Jude that they turned the grace of God into lasciviousness.
point_summary: Jude's warning that false teachers turn the grace of God into lasciviousness—unbridled lust—is cited as the biblical term defining the gross immorality characteristic of perilous times.

context: in Jude, that we're to earnestly contend for the faith that was once delivered unto the saints
point_summary: Earnest contention for the faith once delivered to the saints is the standard by which false teachers—present in every era—are exposed and measured.

context: Jude the servant of Jesus Christ, by the way, it's a good title, to be the servant of Jesus Christ. And brother of James, to them that are sanctified by God the Father and preserved in Jesus Christ and called.
point_summary: Jude's opening address to those "sanctified, preserved, and called" frames the letter's context of shared salvation before its urgent warning against false teaching.

context: Beloved, when I gave all diligence to write unto you of the common salvation... it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: Jude's divine redirection to write about contending for the faith illustrates that sound doctrine is not optional but urgently necessary, delivered once and worth defending.

context: Jude says, I was desiring to write on this. This is what I planned to write on, but it was needful for me to write unto you and to exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered on the saints.
point_summary: Jude's divine redirection—from writing about common salvation to urgently exhorting believers to contend for the once-delivered faith—illustrates that sound doctrine must be defended against false teachers who infiltrate undetected.

context: Why? Verse four, for there are certain men crept in. Do you notice that word unawares?
point_summary: False teachers infiltrate undetected by adopting orthodox vocabulary while redefining its meaning, making vigilant contention for the once-delivered faith urgently necessary.

context: Jude says that we're to earnestly contend for the faith that was once delivered unto the saints
point_summary: Jude's call to contend for the faith once delivered is applied as a generational mandate: each generation bears responsibility to pass the body of received doctrine to the next.

context: Jude warns of that very thing, that the reason that he's writing that short but powerful epistle is that there were certain men that crept in unawares, that had turned the grace of God into lasciviousness.
point_summary: Jude's warning about false teachers who "crept in unawares" illustrates the enemy's strategy of infiltrating the church through deceptive, insider-seeming means.

context: And in Jude verse number 11, the Bible refers to it as being the error of Balaam.
point_summary: Jude 1:11's phrase "the error of Balaam" is one of three biblical designations—alongside "the way of Balaam" (2 Pet. 2:15) and "the doctrine of Balaam" (Rev. 2:14)—identifying the same corrupting teaching condemned in Pergamos.

context: Jude is writing to get them to earnestly contend for the faith
point_summary: Jude's call to contend for the faith is linked here to his warning that false teachers had twisted grace into a license for unbridled lust, illustrating the danger of antinomianism.

context: it was needful in verse number 3 that Jude says it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: Jude 1:3's call to earnestly contend for the faith once delivered to the saints grounds the charge to hold fast to received truth rather than seeking new revelation or cultural relevance.

context: it's interesting in the book of Jude you don't need to turn over there but it's interesting that Michael the archangel in Jude verse number 9 it says yet Michael the archangel when contending with the devil he disputed about the body of Moses durst not bring against him a railing accusation but
point_summary: Even Michael the archangel, in direct conflict with the devil, did not rely on his own authority but deferred to God's power, illustrating the danger of self-confidence in spiritual warfare.

context: said the Lord rebuke thee that's Michael the archangel having this this dispute with with the devil and he did not bring against him a railing accusation but simply said the Lord rebuke thee oh what great lesson for us from that example in the book of Jude
point_summary: Even Michael the archangel, when disputing with the devil, refused to rely on his own authority and instead deferred to God—illustrating that no creature, however exalted, can battle Satan in self-reliance.

context: the one that's crept in unawares, according to Jude 1
point_summary: Jude 1:4's warning about those who "crept in unawares" illustrates that false professors can mimic outward obedience, but lack the consistent pattern that marks genuine possession of saving faith.

context: Jude gives this charge, that we're to earnestly contend for the faith that was once delivered to the saints.
point_summary: Jude's charge to earnestly contend for the faith once delivered underscores that the church receives, proves, and holds fast an already-complete truth—not seeking something new.

context: The Bible tells us in Jude 4, and just a few pages over from 1 John, it's a short letter, only 25 verses here in the book of Jude, but you find that the Bible tells us in Jude 4,
point_summary: Satan's twisting of grace into license (lasciviousness) is identified as a distortion of a genuine biblical truth that hinders the effectiveness of God's people.

context: find that it says in Jude 4 that they've turned the grace of God into lasciviousness.
point_summary: Jude 1:4 is cited to identify the specific distortion false teachers introduce—twisting God's grace into license for unbridled lust rather than a call to righteousness.

context: this idea in Jude where they've turned the grace of God into lasciviousness
point_summary: Turning grace into lasciviousness, as condemned in Jude, stands in direct contradiction to Titus 2's teaching that grace instructs believers to live godly, not to sin freely.

context: Jude makes this statement only one chapter in the book of Jude, but we find that he says this in v. 3, that it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: The enemy's strategy of stealth—creeping in unawares—is drawn from Jude 1:3 to illustrate why earnest contention for the faith is necessary against deceptive opposition.

context: even as Sodom and Gomorrah
point_summary: Sodom and Gomorrah's destruction is cited as a divinely intended example, confirming that God's judgment on ungodliness is not merely historical but a standing warning to all subsequent generations.

context: and the cities about them in like manner, giving themselves over to fornication and going after strange flesh, are set forth for an example, suffering the vengeance of eternal life.
point_summary: Sodom and Gomorrah's destruction is presented in Jude 1:7 as a standing example of divine judgment on ungodly living that people ignore at their peril.

context: Remember the text in Jude? The example of Sodom and Gomorrah
point_summary: Sodom and Gomorrah's destruction, referenced in Jude as a standing example of divine judgment, is invoked here to underscore that unrepentant national sin invites the same overthrow God brought on those cities.

context: the example of Sodom and Gomorrah in verse number 7
point_summary: Sodom and Gomorrah, cited in Jude 7, serves as a warning example of divine judgment against the shameless, celebrated wickedness the preacher sees mirrored in contemporary society.

context: Jude references this in verses 14 and 15. When the Bible says this, and Enoch, also the seventh from Adam, prophesied of these saying, Behold, the Lord cometh with ten thousands of his saints.
point_summary: Enoch's ancient prophecy, cited in Jude 14, declares that Christ will return with ten thousands of his saints—a second coming ignored by a society that flaunts wickedness without shame.

context: Jude references this in verses 14 and 15. When the Bible says this, and Enoch, also the seventh from Adam, prophesied of these saying, Behold, the Lord cometh with ten thousands of his saints.
point_summary: Enoch's ancient prophecy, cited in Jude 15, declares that Christ's return in judgment with ten thousands of his saints stands as a truth largely unknown in contemporary society.

context: the Lord comes with ten thousand of his saints
point_summary: Enoch's ancient prophecy, cited in Jude 14–15, is presented as foretelling the second coming of Christ, when He returns with His saints to execute judgment on the ungodly.

context: To execute judgment upon all, and to convince all that are ungodly among them of all their ungodly deeds
point_summary: Enoch's ancient prophecy, cited in Jude 1:15, declares that Christ's second coming will be an act of judgment executed upon all ungodly deeds and speeches against him.

context: You can read the short book of Jude.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'Beloved, when I gave all diligence to write unto you 'of the common salvation, 'it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you 'that you should earnestly contend for the faith.' And then he says this, 'which was once delivered unto the saints.'
point_summary: Faith is not merely described as unfeigned but must be actively delivered to the next generation, as Jude's phrase "once delivered unto the saints" frames the intentional passing of faith across generations.

context: but here's what Jude said, that you would earnestly contend for the faith that was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: Faithfully contending for the faith once delivered to the saints, as Jude commands, means actively fighting against the evil influences that threaten to capture the next generation.

context: Jude says in verse 22, and of some have compassion making a difference
point_summary: Jude's call to "have compassion, making a difference" frames the entire sermon's burden: that believers must actively engage the lost with gospel urgency.

context: others save with fear, pulling them out of the fire, hating even the garment spotted by the flesh
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: have compassion, making a difference
point_summary: Jude's call to "have compassion, making a difference" is grounded in Christ's own pattern of being moved with compassion toward the multitudes, which then issued in redemptive action.

context: And we notice in verse number 23, and others save with fear pulling them out of the fire, hating even the garment spotted by the flesh.
point_summary: Jude 1:23's call to "save with fear, pulling them out of the fire" frames urgent evangelism as a rescue operation demanding both compassion and bold action toward those perishing.

context: Beloved, when I gave all diligence to write unto you of the common salvation, it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith, notice the statement, which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: Jude's phrase "once delivered unto the saints" is cited to underscore that the biblical record is a completed, reliable deposit entrusted to believers, for which gratitude is owed.

context: Jude says of some having compassion, making a difference
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: there were men, certain men, that had crept in unawares, he tells us that one of the things that these false teachers did is that they were turning the grace of our God into lasciviousness
point_summary: False teachers, Jude warns, were perverting God's grace into unbridled lust, making right understanding of grace urgently necessary.

context: Jude will write in that short epistle and he says that we're to earnestly contend for the faith that was once delivered to the saints.
point_summary: Jude's call to earnestly contend for the faith once delivered to the saints is cited as scriptural grounding for the sermon's insistence that sound doctrine matters and must be held fast.

context: We would find that the admonition that we read of from Jude in verses 3 and 4 is an admonition that we need to apply in our lives today.
point_summary: Jude's urgent call to "earnestly contend for the faith once delivered to the saints" is presented as the necessary response to an ongoing battle for truth that has raged since Genesis 3.

context: We would find that the admonition that we read of from Jude in verses 3 and 4 is an admonition that we need to apply in our lives today.
point_summary: Jude 1:4 is cited to explain why believers must earnestly contend for the faith: false teachers have secretly infiltrated the church, making the battle for truth an urgent, unavoidable necessity.

context: and he says in verse number 3, Beloved, when I gave all diligence to write
point_summary: Jude's urgent call to "earnestly contend for the faith once delivered to the saints" is presented as the necessary response to an ongoing battle for truth that has raged since Genesis 3.

context: unto you of the common salvation, notice it says it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: Jude's interrupted intention to write about common salvation became instead an urgent call to earnestly contend for the faith, because false teachers had already crept in to corrupt it.

context: For there are certain men crept in unawares, who were before of old ordained to this condemnation ungodly men, turning the grace of our God into lasciviousness, and denying the only Lord God and our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Jude 1:4 is cited as the reason earnest contention for the faith is necessary: false teachers had crept in, perverting grace into a license for unbridled lust and denying Christ.

context: ...But I want you to see from Jude 22, so many people say, can God...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And if some have compassion, making a difference.
point_summary: Jude 1:22 is cited as biblical warrant that God can use any individual—like Oded—to make a difference through compassion and courage in the lives of others.

context: And others, save with fear, pulling them out of the fire, hating even the garment spotted by the flesh.
point_summary: Jude 1:23's call to "save with fear, pulling them out of the fire" grounds the sermon's appeal that God can use any individual, like Oded, to make a courageous, life-changing difference for others.

context: ...even as Sodom and Gomorrah, and the cities about them in like manner, giving themselves over to fornication, and going after strange flesh, are set forth for an example, suffering the vengeance of eternal life.
point_summary: Sodom and Gomorrah's destruction is presented in Jude as a standing historical example of divine judgment against sexual immorality and the pursuit of unnatural flesh, warning of eternal vengeance.

context: In fact, if you read the book of Jude, you understand that Jude says it's necessary for me to write these things unto you, that you earnestly
point_summary: Rejecting the known truth of Christ's atoning work is gravely dangerous, as Jude's warning about false teachers who distort grace and deny Christ illustrates the peril of turning away from what has been clearly received.

context: the false teachers that are creeping in according to the book of Jude, they turn the grace of God into lasciviousness
point_summary: False teachers, as warned in Jude, pervert grace into a license for unbridled lust—the very antithesis of what grace teaches, which is denial of ungodliness.

context: According to the book of Jude, they turn the grace of God into lasciviousness.
point_summary: False teachers pervert God's grace into a license for unbridled lust, in direct contrast to grace's true purpose of teaching holy, self-denying living.

context: Some having compassion, Jude, making a difference
point_summary: Believers are called to mirror Christ's compassion by showing love toward the unsaved, so that those in bondage to sin might recognize both their condition and the freedom available to them.

context: even as we started there in the book of Jude, the faith has been delivered. We have the body of teaching of truth. We need to earnestly contend for the faith
point_summary: The faith once delivered to the saints (Jude 1:3) obligates the present generation to earnestly contend for it by instructing those who follow in the body of Christian truth.

context: those false teachers they were turning the grace of God into lasciviousness unbridled lust
point_summary: False teachers distort grace into a license for unbridled lust, whereas the grace of God actually teaches believers to live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present world.

context: If you think about the book of Jude and we have the truth of God's word
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Bible says in the book of Jude, of some have compassion, making a difference.
point_summary: Jude 1:22's call to "have compassion, making a difference" is applied as a summons to seize intentional opportunities for influence in others' lives, as Jehosheba risked hers to save Joash.

context: Notice if you will in the book of Jude
point_summary: The book of Jude is cited to show that even believers must approach evangelism and the rescue of the perishing with a reverent fear of God, not casual indifference.

context: notice in verse number 22, and if some have compassion, making a difference
point_summary: Jude 1:22 is cited as a bridge to verse 23, where fear-motivated urgency—"pulling them out of the fire"—frames evangelism as a compassionate but sobering rescue from judgment.

context: save with fear, pulling them out of the fire, hating even the garment spotted by the flesh
point_summary: Saving the lost requires not only compassion but also a fear-driven urgency—pulling them from the fire—because a healthy fear of God motivates genuine gospel witness.

context: the Lord rebuke thee, as Moses did and recorded there in the book of Jude
point_summary: When believers rebuke Satan with the words "the Lord rebuke thee," as Moses did according to Jude, they can walk in victory over an enemy who knows his time is short.

context: Jude says this, and Jude only has one chapter, but in verse number three of the book of Jude, it's such an important... important verse of Scripture
point_summary: The faith "once delivered unto the saints" presupposes a faithful prior generation that received and passed on the body of doctrine, establishing the same obligation for the present generation to teach it forward.

context: Beloved, when I gave all diligence to write unto you of the common salvation, it was needful for me to write unto you and exhort you that you should earnestly contend for the faith. Then notice what it says here, which was once delivered unto the saints.
point_summary: The phrase "once delivered unto the saints" is read as evidence that a prior faithful generation was entrusted with a body of doctrine and obligated to pass it intact to the next.
